LESSONS learned​

EEME20002​

LESSONS LEARNED: REGISTER​

Ref.​

Lesson​

Source​

Interested Parties​

Dissemination​

1​

Importance of in-person groupwork​

Non-ideal attendance to early sessions​

All members of group​

Notification via WhatsApp & discussion in-person​

2​

Use of collaborative workspaces (i.e. GitHub)​

Disorganisation of project files​

All members of group​

Creation of GitHub repositories and agreed policy to publish work​

4​

Parallelising workflow between group members​

Command team workflow​

Command team​

Decision to redesign the FSM with workflow in mind​

5​

Collaboration between group members when working remotely​

Data processing team workflow​

Data Processing team​

Decision to work through one branch as a shared workspace​

LESSONS LEARNED: CONT.​

  • Parallelising workflow relies on a good plan: you must know how you want to stitch each piece together before you split into individual tasks.​

  • We ran up against this problem when working on the command processor. It was difficult to divvy up tasks because our logic relied on one single state machine.​

  • To prevent this, we chose to block up the FSM design so that each of us could work on a small section, whilst maintaining compatibility between parts of the code.​

​

bristol.ac.uk/<add url>​

LESSONS LEARNED: CONT.​

  • The data processing team also found this to be a challenge when working remotely, outside of labs. It was difficult to collaborate on code when working on separate branches of the same project, when this was not an issue for the design of the FSM.​

  • The workflow could be merged into a single branch to tackle the single state machine design, and the coding of each state assigned to each group member.​

  • This would allow a collaborative, parallelised workflow to get the job done more quickly and easily.​

​

bristol.ac.uk/<add url>​

IP REGISTER:​

Ref.​

Type​

Source​

Description​

Limitations on Use​

IP-CMD-1​

Foreground: Algorithmic Logic “Sliding Window” Hexadecimal Formatter​

Command Processor Design Team​

Digital hardware model: ​

A space-optimised algorithm for converting raw binary bytes into ASCII-formatted hex chars, replacing 20+ states with only 3.​

Royalty-free, non-exclusive license for internal R&D use by group members. Commercial/Third-party use requires separate agreement.​

IP-DAT-1​

Foreground:​

Real-time Streaming Peak-Detection Algorithm​

Data Processor Design​

Team​

Digital hardware model:​

Evaluates incoming data against a stored maximum, dynamically updates the peak value and index​

Royalty-free, non-exclusive license for internal R&D use by group members. Commercial/Third-party use requires separate agreement.​
